question.text: An investor purchases 5,000 units of a mutual fund scheme at a NAV of Rs. 20.00 per unit. After 6 months, the investor redeems all units when the NAV is Rs. 24.00. The scheme charges an exit load of 1% for redemptions within 1 year. What is the total redemption amount the investor receives?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Repurchase Price = NAV - Exit Load = Rs. 24.00 - (Rs. 24.00 x 1%) = Rs. 24.00 - Rs. 0.24 = Rs. 23.76. Total Redemption Amount = 5,000 units x Rs. 23.76 = Rs. 1,18,800. Exit load applies because redemption is within 1 year.
**The Trap:** Rs. 1,20,000 ignores exit load entirely (5,000 x Rs. 24.00). Rs. 1,19,000 may come from applying exit load on the purchase NAV instead of redemption NAV (5,000 x Rs. 23.80 = Rs. 1,19,000 — wrong because exit load is always on redemption NAV). Rs. 99,000 applies exit load on the purchase price (5,000 x Rs. 19.80) which is completely wrong.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Exit load is always calculated on the applicable NAV at the time of redemption, not on the purchase NAV. This distinction is critical and tested in NISM V-A and V-B.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always calculate the exact redemption amount for clients factoring in exit load. A common mistake investors make is assuming exit load applies on their invested amount. The exit load amount goes back to the scheme (credited to the scheme), not to the AMC, which benefits remaining unitholders.
